Author: Stephan Jahrling
Title: The Self-Healing Dev Loop for Mobile Apps
Text:
The Self-Healing Dev Loop for Mobile Apps
A real experiment with ralph.sh, Claude Code, React Native, Expo, Jest and Maestro.
I was on a train to Berlin, early December 2025, on my way to meet my new team as Engineering Team Lead, when I stumbled upon a Medium article about something called ralph.sh — a system for running autonomous AI agents on real codebases. Not “paste a prompt, get a snippet.” An agent that takes a product requirements document, breaks it into tasks, writes code, generates tests, runs them, fixes its own bugs, and commits to Git. Autonomously. In a loop.
I bookmarked it. Told myself I’d try it. Then life happened, and six weeks passed.
At the end of January 2026, Anthropic dropped two pieces on their engineering blog that cleared my calendar. First: a deep-dive on building effective harnesses for long-running agents. Second: a case study of an autonomous agent that had built a functioning C compiler — running for days, writing and debugging code with no human intervention.
That was the moment I thought: I’m doing this today.
The System Behind the Experiment
Let me be clear about something upfront — because credibility matters. When I say “ralph.sh,” I’m not talking about an official Anthropic product. I used a community implementation by JeredBlu — a set of prompts, skills, and conventions built on top of Claude Code, Anthropic’s CLI tool for running Claude directly in your terminal.
The setup idea is straightforward: give the agent a well-structured PRD, and it works through it — task by task, test by test, commit by commit. What makes this fundamentally different from “ChatGPT writes code” is the closed loop. The agent doesn’t just generate code — it writes tests, runs them, evaluates results, fixes its own errors, and commits with a descriptive message. It operates inside your repository like a developer on your team, not like a chatbot in a separate window.
Most demos online show web apps. A Next.js landing page, a CRUD dashboard. I wanted to stress-test the system, so I went mobile: React Native, Expo, a real Android emulator, real build toolchains with Java, Gradle, and Xcode. My hypothesis was simple — if this loop is truly robust, it shouldn’t matter whether it builds a web page or a mobile app.
The app: a Mantra App for daily positive affirmations — swipeable Tinder-style cards, categories, favorites, CRUD operations, custom categories with color pickers, and Clerk authentication. Enough moving parts to be a real test.
Preparing the Ground
Here’s what most tutorials skip: autonomous agents can only be as autonomous as the infrastructure allows. If the agent can’t test its own work, it’s just a code generator with extra steps. Self-healing requires a surface to heal against.
Before the agent wrote a single line of app code, I set up the test infrastructure manually:
- Expo with React Native and the default app template
- Jest + React Native Testing Library for unit and component tests
- Maestro for end-to-end UI flows on the Android emulator
- A clean Git repository with an initial commit as a baseline
Then came the sandbox configuration — defining exactly which commands the agent could run, which file paths it could access, and which network domains were allowed. I permitted git commit
but blocked git push
(manual approval only). I locked out .env
files, SSH keys, sudo
, and rm-rf
. Tuning this .claude/settings.json
took longer than any individual feature:
{
"env": {
"XDG_CACHE_HOME": ".cache",
"npm_config_cache": ".cache/npm",
},
"permissions": {
"allow": [
"WebFetch(domain:*)",
"Bash(npm run:*)",
"Bash(npm test:*)",
"Bash(npm install:*)",
"Bash(npm run dev:*)",
"Bash(npm run build:*)",
"Bash(npm run lint:*)",
"Bash(npx:*)",
"Bash(pnpm:*)",
"Bash(yarn:*)",
"Bash(bun:*)",
"Bash(git diff:*)",
"Bash(git status:*)",
"Bash(git log:*)",
"Bash(git add:*)",
"Bash(git commit:*)",
"Bash(npx run android:*)",
],
"deny": [
"Bash(sudo:*)",
"Bash(docker:*)",
"Bash(rm -rf:*)",
"Read(~/.ssh/**)",
"Read(~/.aws/**)",
"Read(**/.env)",
"Read(**/.env.*)",
"Edit(**/.env)",
"Edit(**/.env.*)"
],
"ask": ["Bash(git push:*)"],
"defaultMode": "acceptEdits"
},
"sandbox": {
"enabled": true,
"autoAllowBashIfSandboxed": true,
"allowUnsandboxedCommands": true,
"excludedCommands": ["maestro", "java"],
"network": {
"allowLocalBinding": true,
"allowedDomains": [
"registry.npmjs.org",
"www.npmjs.com",
"www.npmjs.org",
"npmjs.com",
"npmjs.org"
]
}
}
}
I built this PRD with a Claude Code skill that asked me targeted questions — data model, navigation structure, color schemes, testing strategy. The AI helped me articulate the vision precisely. But the vision was mine.
Watching It Work
I used Claude Code on the CLI — Claude Opus 4.5 for the first run, then Opus 4.6 when it was released mid-experiment. The agent receives the PRD and a PROMPT.md
defining its operating loop: read current state → find next open task → implement → run tests → verify with Maestro → log in activity.md
→ flip passes
to true
→ commit → repeat.
For my first run, I limited it to 3 tasks. I wanted to watch the loop in action before trusting it with the full scope.
In 90 minutes: the complete data model — TypeScript interfaces, a storage service, a React Context provider, and 36 seed mantras across 6 categories. 37 unit tests. All passing. Commit. Tab navigation rebuilt: three tabs, new screens, icon mappings, Expo boilerplate cleared. 38 tests passing. Commit. MantraCard
with Tinder-style gestures, Reanimated 4 animations, and haptic feedback. 47 tests. Commit.
No intervention. No prompts from me. No debugging on my end.
After those three tasks, I let it run. Final result: 20 completed tasks. 301 Jest tests across 17 suites — all passing. Maestro E2E tests for every major flow. Zero ESLint errors. Zero TypeScript errors. 20 commits. The app ships swipeable cards, category management, full CRUD, favorites, Clerk auth with sign-up, login, profile editing, profile picture upload, color gradients, entrance animations, and haptic feedback across every screen.
After every task, the agent wrote standup-style notes to activity.md
— what it built, what commands it ran, what problems it hit. Automatically.
I didn’t write a single line of code.
What Went Wrong
It wasn’t flawless. The biggest friction wasn’t code quality — it was the environment. Mobile development has deep system dependencies, and my sandbox config blocked several of them.
Maestro and the sandbox. Maestro requires Java and Gradle. The sandbox’s default process and network restrictions blocked both. The fix was adding selective permission exceptions — but finding the right ones required multiple failed runs and inspecting sandbox violation logs. If you’re starting with autonomous agents, start with a web app. The agent wrote excellent React Native code. The infrastructure around mobile was the bottleneck.
Screenshot upload limits. Maestro generates full-resolution emulator screenshots for the agent’s visual verification step. At default emulator resolution, the images exceeded Claude’s upload limit and caused silent failures in the loop. Fix: use an emulator with a low resolution. Only discoverable through trial and error.
Flaky async tests. Mid-experiment, Jest tests across 17 parallel suites started failing intermittently — async initialization timing. The agent diagnosed the root cause ( waitFor
thresholds too low for parallel execution), increased the timeouts, and moved on. It fixed its own problem without asking for help. That self-healing behavior is the actual differentiator from every other AI coding tool I’ve used.
What This Changes
Three technical skills matter more now than they did a year ago.
Specification writing. The PRD is the new leverage point — typed data models, explicit task boundaries, concrete acceptance criteria. The agent’s output quality correlates directly with how precisely the spec was written. A vague task produces vague code. A well-scoped task with clear steps and testable outcomes produces production-ready components.
Test architecture. The self-healing loop only works if the test surface is accurate. Flaky tests confuse the agent; it chases false failures. Missing coverage means broken features slip through undetected. The developer who sets up Jest configuration, writes the first Maestro flows, and keeps the test suite reliable is the one enabling everything that follows.
Agent orchestration. Knowing when to let the agent run and when to intervene is a skill that didn’t exist two years ago. So is reading an activity.md
log to understand what the agent actually decided — and why a particular implementation diverged from the spec.
What I’m watching next: Anthropic’s multi-agent collaboration features. The same loop, but with specialized agents — one for feature development, one for testing, one for code review — running in parallel on the same repo. Still early and unstable. But the direction is clear.
—
20 tasks. 301 tests. 20 commits. A few hours of agent runtime. Zero lines of code written by me.
If you’re a developer or tech lead curious about autonomous agents — don’t just read about it. Set up a repo, write a PRD (also with help of an agent), configure Claude Code with ralph.sh, and press start. Then tell me what you build.
—
